title: How Does the Generator Learn?
concept: The generator's loss is based purely on how often it fools the discriminator — it never sees real images directly, only a gradient signal routed backward through the discriminator's judgment.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What loss function trains the discriminator, and what are its two possible target labels?

options.text: Binary Cross-Entropy, with target 1 for real images and 0 for fake images
Mean Squared Error comparing pixel colors directly
A three-way loss with real, fake, and unknown labels

exercises.explain: Correct! The discriminator's loss is BCE with target 1 (real) or 0 (fake).

cards.title: Learning to draw with your eyes closed
cards.body: Imagine trying to learn to draw a realistic apple, but you're never allowed to look at a real apple. All you get is a single thumbs-up or thumbs-down from a judge after every attempt. That's exactly the position the generator is in.

cards.title: The Generator's Loss
cards.body: The generator's loss is NOT based on comparing its output to real images pixel-by-pixel. Instead, it's based on how successfully its fake images fool the discriminator — specifically, how close the discriminator's real-probability p on a fake image gets to 1 (fooled) instead of 0 (caught).

cards.title: Blindfolded sculptor, one verdict
cards.body: Picture a sculptor working blindfolded, who never gets to touch a real statue for reference. After each attempt, an assistant simply reports "the judge is 80% convinced" or "only 10% convinced." The sculptor adjusts based purely on that single number climbing or falling — never on seeing the real thing.

cards.title: Same score, opposite goal
cards.body: Say the generator makes a fake image and the discriminator outputs p = 0.2 — only 20% convinced it's real, so the fake was caught. From the discriminator's side this is a *good* result (low loss, target 0). From the generator's side it's the opposite: it wanted that p pushed toward 1.0, so it treats p = 0.2 as a high generator loss and adjusts in the direction that would have raised it.

cards.title: No access to real pixels, ever
cards.body: You might assume the generator improves by comparing its fakes to real training images directly, the way you'd check a copy against an original. In reality, the generator's weights are never updated using real image data at all — every gradient it receives is routed backward through the discriminator's verdict.

left.text:
Sees real training images
Sees the generator's fake images
Learns from both, directly

right.text:
Never sees real images
Only sees its random noise input
Learns only via the discriminator's score

exercises.prompt: Why can't the generator use a simple 'compare pixels to a real photo' loss like ordinary supervised learning?

options.text: It never has a matching real image to compare against — it only gets the discriminator's verdict
Pixel comparisons are too slow to compute on a GPU
Generators don't use gradients at all
Real images are deleted immediately after the discriminator trains

exercises.explain: Correct — there's no paired real image for any given fake, so pixel comparison isn't possible.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a high discriminator confidence that a fake image is real (p close to 1) is a GOOD outcome for the generator.
exercises.explain: True. Fooling the discriminator is exactly the generator's goal, so a high p means low generator loss.

exercises.sentence: The generator's only training signal is the ___ that flows backward through the discriminator's judgment.
exercises.explain: No real images ever reach the generator's weights directly — only a gradient computed from the discriminator's output.

exercises.prompt: Which of these correctly describes how the generator's loss is calculated?

options.text: From how convincingly its fakes fooled the discriminator — never from comparing to a real photo
From the average brightness difference between fake and real images
From counting how many pixels exactly match a reference photo

exercises.explain: Correct — the generator's loss is entirely about the discriminator's verdict, not pixel similarity.

exercises.prompt: A generator produces a fake face. The discriminator scores it p = 0.15 — mostly unconvinced. What does the generator's loss signal tell it to do next?

options.text: Push its weights in whatever direction would have raised that 0.15 closer to 1.0 — even though it never sees a real face to compare against
Copy the pixels of the nearest real training image exactly
Ignore the score since it's below 0.5
Lower its own confidence in the discriminator's judgment

exercises.explain: Exactly — the generator only ever chases the discriminator's score upward. Next: how these two loss functions coexist in a single training loop without collapsing into one.
